coredata.dc:title: Sensitive determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry using carbon dots synthesized from a non-essential amino acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sinduja, B.
Abraham John, S.
coredata.dc:description: The present study describes the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using a non-essential amino acid, asparagine as a precursor. The HR-TEM image shows that the size of the prepared CDs was 2.9±0.2nm with a spherical morphology. The UV–visible spectrum of CDs exhibits a major band at 307nm along with a shoulder band around 207nm corresponding to n-π* and π-π* transitions, respectively. Further, the CDs show emission maximum at 441nm when excited at 348nm. The synthesized CDs were then exploited for the determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry based on the decrease in emission intensity at 441nm. It was found that emission intensity of CDs at 441nm was decreased while adding 0.5μM rutin to CDs. On the other hand, addition of other metal ions and anions including 5mM Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, NO3 − and oxalate, 2.5mM Cu2+ and Fe3+ and 3mM glycine, glucose, histidine, proline and cysteine does not affect the emission intensity at 441nm. A good linearity was observed for the emission intensity against 0.5–15μM rutin with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 and the limit of detection was found to be 1×10−7 M (61μg/L) (S/N=3). The real sample analysis was done by determining rutin in a pharmaceutical sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Asparagine
Pyrolysis
Carbon dots
Rutin
Fluorescence
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CDs by UV–Visible, HR-TEM, Spectrofluorimetry and XRD
Characterization of CDs by FT-IR Spectroscopy and XPS
Selective Determination of Rutin in the Presence of Possible Interferences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra of (a) asparagine and (b) CDs. Inset: (a) asparagine and (b) CDs under UV illumination.
Emission spectra of CDs with various excitation wavelengths.
FT-IR spectrum of CDs synthesized from asparagine (a) and rutin (b).
XPS deconvoluted profile of (a) O 1s, (b) N 1s and (d) C 1s region.

Emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of rutin: (a) 0, (b) 0.5, (c) 1, (d) 1.5, (e) 2, (f) 2.5, (g) 3, (h) 3.5, (i) 4, (j) 4.5, (k) 5, (l) 6, (m) 7, (n) 8, (o) 9, (p) 10 and (q) 15μM. Inset: Calibration plot of emission intensity against the concentrations of rutin and photographs of CDs in the (A) absence and (B) presence of rutin under UV illumination.
Spectral overlap of absorbance spectrum of rutin and emission spectrum of CDs.

CDs in the presence of 5mM of Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, OX (oxalate) and NO3, 2.5mM Cu2+ and Fe3+ and 3mM of glycine (Gly), glucose (Glu), histidine (His), proline (Pro) and cysteine (cys) and 5μM of rutin (RTN).
Mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of CDs by rutin.

head.ce:title: Sensitive determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry using carbon dots synthesized from a non-essential amino acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute, Gandhigram 624 302, Dindigul, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study describes the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using a non-essential amino acid, asparagine as a precursor. The HR-TEM image shows that the size of the prepared CDs was 2.9±0.2nm with a spherical morphology. The UV–visible spectrum of CDs exhibits a major band at 307nm along with a shoulder band around 207nm corresponding to n-π* and π-π* transitions, respectively. Further, the CDs show emission maximum at 441nm when excited at 348nm. The synthesized CDs were then exploited for the determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry based on the decrease in emission intensity at 441nm. It was found that emission intensity of CDs at 441nm was decreased while adding 0.5μM rutin to CDs. On the other hand, addition of other metal ions and anions including 5mM Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, NO3 − and oxalate, 2.5mM Cu2+ and Fe3+ and 3mM glycine, glucose, histidine, proline and cysteine does not affect the emission intensity at 441nm. A good linearity was observed for the emission intensity against 0.5–15μM rutin with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 and the limit of detection was found to be 1×10−7 M (61μg/L) (S/N=3). The real sample analysis was done by determining rutin in a pharmaceutical sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon based quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are new class of materials have received much attention in recent years mainly due to their interesting optical properties. CDs are carbon nanoparticles of size less than 10nm in diameter whereas GQDs are graphene sheets of lateral size less than 100nm [1–3]. They possess excellent physical and chemical properties including chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high stability and solubility, resistance to photobleaching, intense fluorescence, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [4–7]. Further, they have several advantages over organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in various aspects. In contrast to organic dyes, CDs show unique optical properties having strong fluorescence with wide excitation and emission spectral range besides strong resistant to photobleaching. On the other hand, semiconductor quantum dots prepared from Cd, Se and Te are toxic and mainly synthesized in organic phase besides high cost. Besides, the preparation of CDs is not as complicated as the preparation of semiconductor quantum dots.

Several methods are available in the literature for the preparation of CDs which include microwave, ultrasonic, hydrothermal, electrochemical, acidic oxidation and laser ablation [8]. Numerous precursors are used for the preparation of CDs namely glucose, MWCNTs, sucrose, EDTA, fullerenes, ascorbic acid and plant materials [8]. The optical and fluorescence properties of CDs arise from quantum confinement and edge effects [9]. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs also depends upon the presence of surface functional groups [9]. Any alteration on the surface of the CDs leads to change in the fluorescence. Taking this advantage, CDs have been extensively used as chemosensor for several toxic metal ions because of their strong interaction with the surface functional groups of CDs. But, the exact mechanism for the fluorescence property of CDs is yet to be established. Most of the researchers proposed that the emission property of CDs is due to the radiative recombination of surface electrons and holes [8,9].Recently, several papers have been published for the detection of toxic metal ions and other molecules based on the optical property of CDs which include nitrite [10], phosphate [11], glucose [12], Hg(II) [13,14] and Fe(III) [15,16]. Further, CDs were utilized in cellular bioimaging [17], light energy conversion [18], photovoltaic device [19], anticancer therapy and drug delivery systems [20].

Rutin, a natural flavone derivative is considered as one of the most bioactive flavonoids and also called as vitamin P [21]. It is present in 70 herbal remedies. It is also used as a therapeutical medicine with functions in a wide range of circulatory problems which can decrease blood pressure and reduce capillary permeability [22]. Further, it exhibits wide physiological functions like anti-inflammatory, antibacterial and antioxidant. Besides, many health foods and different beverages usually contain rutin [21]. Due to these advantages, rutin was used in both medicine and human diet [23]. Variety of analytical methods was employed for the determination of rutin which include chemiluminescence [24,25], sequential injection analysis [26,27], capillary electrophoresis spectrophotometry [28,29] and electrochemical methods [30–33]. Although these methods have been effectively used for the determination of rutin, they have several demerits such high cost, less sensitivity, involve complicated procedure and required trained personnel. On the other hand, spectrofluorimetry method is very simple, less cost, easy to handle and more sensitive. To the best of our knowledge, no report is available in the literature for the determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry. Thus, the objective of the present study is to synthesize CDs from a non-essential amino acid precursor, l-asparagine and utilize them for the determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry. The CDs were synthesized from asparagine by pyrolysis method and were characterized by different spectral, HR-TEM and XRD techniques. The formed CDs were bluish green luminescence in nature and show emission maximum at 441nm when excited at 348nm. The addition of rutin to CDs decreases the emission intensity at 441nm. Based on the decrease in emission intensity, the concentration of rutin was determined.

l-Asparagine was purchased from Sigma. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ferrous sulphate (FeSO4) were purchased from Central Drug House (CDH) and were used as received. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) was purchased from FISCHER and iron (II) chloride (FeCl2) was purchased from MERCK. Copper sulphate (CuSO4) from SISCO and rutin from Alpha Aesar and were used as received. Histidine, proline, cysteine and glycine were purchased from SRL and glucose from HIMEDIA. Double distilled water was used to prepare all the solutions.

Absorption spectra were measured by using JASCO V-550 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectral measurements were performed on a JASCO FP-8500 spectrofluorimeter equipped with a xenon discharge lamp. The FT-IR measurements were carried out in JASCO FT-IR 460 plus model. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of CDs were obtained from a JEOL JEM 2100 Advanced Analytical HR-TEM, operating at 200kV. For the TEM measurement, the sample was prepared by dropping 2μL CDs on the carbon coated copper grid. X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out with a Rigaku X-ray diffraction unit using Ni–filtered Cu Kα (λ=1.5406Å) radiation.

The CDs were prepared as follows. 0.5g of asparagine was taken in the round bottom flask and heated to 100°C in a heating mantle. The solid was melted within 10min and the color of the melted liquid was changed from colorless to brown after 20min. The obtained brown color liquid was then added dropwise into 100mL of NaOH (10mg/mL) with constant stirring and then finally stored in a refrigerator.

In a 10mL flask, a known amount of rutin (II) was added to CDs. 2.5mL of each solution was transferred to the quartz cell and the absorbance was measured and the same procedure was used for emission measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs by UV–Visible, HR-TEM, Spectrofluorimetry and XRD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the absorption spectra recorded for asparagine and CDs. The absorption spectrum of asparagine shows a band at 209nm (curve a). On the other hand, CDs show absorption band at 307nm along with a shoulder band around 207nm, corresponding to n-π* and π-π* transitions, respectively (curve b). The inset photographs show asparagine and CDs under UV irradiation. The asparagine does not show luminescence (photograph a) whereas CDs show strong bluish green luminescence (photograph b). This confirms the successful formation of CDs. Fig. S1a and b show the HR-TEM image of CDs and particle size distribution. The HR-TEM image shows that CDs were spherical in shape and the average particle size was found to be 2.9±0.2nm.

Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. While exciting CDs at 300, 310, 320, 330, 340, 345, 348, 350 and 360nm, they show emission at 434, 433, 433, 434, 435, 438, 441, 441 and 441nm, respectively. This indicates that while varying the excitation wavelength no significant change in the emission maximum was observed for the CDs. But, the emission intensity varies with respect to excitation wavelength, suggesting that the size and surface states of CDs are almost uniform [9]. The CDs exhibit maximum intensity at 441nm while exciting at 348nm. Therefore, 348nm was fixed as an excitation wavelength for all the fluorescence measurements. The excitation and the corresponding emission spectra are shown in Fig. S2.

The CDs were coated on the ITO plate for XRD measurement and the corresponding XRD pattern is shown in Fig. S3. It illustrates the diffraction features appearing at 26.3°, 33.39°, 37.7° and 39.56° due to graphitic carbon planes of C(002), C(111), C(020) and C(021), respectively. The obtained three tagged peaks correspond to ITO substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs by FT-IR Spectroscopy and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectrum recorded for CDs is shown in Fig. 3a. The precursor, asparagine contains amide, amine and carboxylic acid groups (inset of Fig. 3a). Therefore, the CDs synthesized from asparagine may also be expected to contain amine, amide and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs shows sharp bands at 1631 and 1740cm−1 corresponding to carbonyl stretching of amide and carboxylic acid functionalities, respectively. Along with carbonyl of amide, a band at 1465cm−1 responsible for NH deformation mode of NH bending was appeared. The band appeared at 1380cm−1 was attributed to the OH bending of carboxylic acid whereas 1038cm−1 band corresponds to CN bond stretching of primary amine. The peak at 1105cm−1 was attributed to the CO stretching of carboxylic group. The amine and hydroxyl groups are merged to give a broad band at 3513cm−1. In addition, CH2 stretching bands were appeared at 2920 and 2854cm−1. The obtained FT-IR results confirmed the presence of amide, carboxyl and amine functionalities on the surface of CDs. Fig. 3b shows the FT-IR spectrum of rutin which shows the peak at 3431cm−1 corresponding to OH stretching. The peak at 1655cm−1 is due to CO stretching. The CC of aromatic ring is appeared at 1505cm−1. The peak at 1205cm−1 appeared is attributed to COC stretching. The CH stretching of rutin is appeared at 2934cm−1.

Fig. 4 shows the XPS of CDs. Fig. 4A shows O1s region for different oxygen functional groups which include 531.7 and 533.3eV for COH and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks at 398.6, 399.5 and 400.3eV in N 1s region corresponds to CNC, (C)3 N and NH bonds (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C shows the C 1s spectrum of CDs. It shows peaks at 284.3, 285.3, 286.4 and 288.4eV corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO/CN bonds, respectively.

The fluorescence nature of the CDs was studied by taking CDs in PB solution at different pH (pH3–12). The emission spectra of CDs at different pH solutions are shown in Fig. S4. It can be seen from Fig. S4 that when the pH of the solution increases, no significant change in the emission intensity was observed.

The main objective of the present study is to utilize the synthesized CDs for the determination of rutin. Fig. 5 shows the emission spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of rutin (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4, 4.5, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 and15μM). Curve ‘a’ corresponds to the emission spectrum of CDs with the emission maximum at 441nm. While adding 0.5μM rutin to CDs solution, a decrease in the emission intensity was observed at 441nm (curve b). Further increasing the concentration of rutin from 1 to 10μM, not only the emission intensity decreases but also the emission maximum shifts towards longer wavelength (curves c-p) and it reaches to 459nm after the addition of 15μM rutin (curve q). The emission intensity decreases linearly with the increase in the concentration of rutin with the correlation coefficient of 0.9970 and the limit of detection was found to be 61μg/L. It has been suggested that the fluorescence nature of CDs is mainly attributed to quantum confinement and surface functional groups [2]. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs reveals that amine, amide and carboxyl groups are available in their surface. On the other hand, rutin contains hydroxyl groups. While adding rutin to CDs, carboxyl, amine and amide groups in the surface of the CDs interact with the hydroxyl groups present in the rutin moiety through hydrogen bonding interaction. In addition, π-π interaction from the aromatic stack of CDs and rutin is also possible (Scheme 1 ). These two interactions resulted in the non-radiative emission process through energy transfer. As shown in Fig. 6 , the energy transfer is fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). This is evidenced from the overlapping of absorbance spectrum of rutin and emission spectrum of CDs.

Stern-Volmer plot is shown in Fig. S4. It is not a conventional linear fit of Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where ‘F’ is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of rutin F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and Q is the concentration of rutin. Stern-Volmer quenching constant was found to be 2.7 × 103 Lmol−1. The obtained Stern-Volmer plot suggests that both dynamic and static quenching processes taking place in this system.

The binding constant can be calculated by using the Eq. (2).
(2)
log
F 0
− F /
F 0
= log
K A
+ n
log
Q
where, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs, F is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of rutin, Q is the concentration of rutin, KA is the binding constant and n is the number of binding sites. KA measured from the intercept and slope obtained by plotting a graph between log (F0 −F/F0) and log [Q], respectively. This is shown in Fig. S5. The KA was found to be 5.1×104 Lmol−1. The obtained value suggests the presence of a strong interaction between CDs and rutin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective Determination of Rutin in the Presence of Possible Interferences
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CDs towards rutin was carried out in the presence of different metal ions, anions and physiological compounds. The interfering ions including cations and anions of various concentrations were added into the CDs containing rutin. The addition of 5μM rutin decreases the fluorescence intensity of CDs. But, the addition of 5mM of Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, NO3 − and oxalate (OX), 2.5mM Cu2+ and Fe3+ and 3mM of glycine, glucose, histidine, proline and cysteine does not affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 7 ). The obtained results reveal that CDs can be used to determine rutin even in the presence of 1000-fold excess of Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, NO3 − and OX, 500-fold excess of Cu2+, Fe3+ and 600-fold excess of glycine, glucose, histidine, proline and cysteine. As discussed above, the selectivity of CDs towards rutin is mainly due to the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of rutin and amine, amide and carboxyl groups of CDs besides π-π interaction of the aromatic stack of CDs with rutin.

The practical application of the present method was tested by determining rutin in pharmaceutical sample (BROMEZER tablet). The labeled content of rutin in the tablet was 50mg. The tablet was powdered and dissolved in 10mL of water. 1.22, 2.44 and 3.66mg/L of rutin was added to CDs and it was found to be 1.20, 2.43 and 3.66mg/L, respectively (Table S1). The recoveries of 98.3, 99.5 and 100% were obtained. The obtained good recovery results indicate that CDs can be used for the determination rutin in pharmaceutical samples by spectrofluorimetry.

The present study describes the synthesis of CDs using asparagine as a precursor and utilization of the resulting bluish green luminescent CDs for the determination of rutin. The synthesized CDs were confirmed by UV–vis spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD and spectrofluorimetry. While adding 0.5μM rutin to CDs, the emission intensity at 441nm was decreased. Further, increasing the concentration of rutin, the emission intensity systematically decreased. The decreased emission intensity at 441nm was attributed to the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl and amine functional groups of CDs and rutin, respectively and π-π interaction between the aromatic stacks. Further, CDs selectively determined rut in the presence of 1000-fold excess of Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, NO3 − and oxalate, 500-fold excess of Cu2+ and Fe3+ and 600-fold excess of glycine, glucose, histidine, proline and cysteine. The limit of detection was found to 1×10−7 M (S/N=3). The practical application of the present method was demonstrated by determining rutin in commercially available tablet.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: B. Sinduja thanks the Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi, for the award of DST-Inspire Junior Research Fellowship (IF150600(2015)). Financial support from Department of Biotechnology (BT/PR10372/PFN/20/904/2013), New Delhi, India is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs, excitation and emission spectra and Stern-Volmer plot are given in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.12.067.
